title: Storage of telecom-band time-bin qubits in thin-film lithium niobate
authors: Xiao-Jie Wang, Yong-Teng Wang, Zi-Wei Zhao, Yong-Min Li, Tian-Shu Yang
abstract: Integrated photonics has emerged as a promising platform for quantum communication and quantum computation. Thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) has gained significant attention in this field due to its exceptional optical properties, enabling the realization of numerous integrated photonic devices. However, quantum memory, which serves as a universal building block for the quantum internet, has not yet been demonstrated in TFLN. In this study, we realized the first on-chip quantum memory using erbium ions doped TFLN. The developed quantum memory achieves a storage time of 400 ns with an efficiency of 1.95%, significantly outperforming conventional waveguide delay lines. The multimode capability is demonstrated by successfully storing four temporal modes. Furthermore, single-photon-level coherent pulses are encoded into time-bin qubits and stored with a fidelity of 96.8% , surpassing the classical limit achievable by measure-and-prepare strategy. Our results demonstrate the first on-chip quantum memory for telecom-band time-bin qubits in TFLN, providing a key building block toward integrated quantum registers and repeaters for scalable quantum information processing.
summary: This paper reports the first implementation of an on-chip quantum memory using erbium-doped thin-film lithium niobate. It achieves high-fidelity storage of telecom-band time-bin qubits and demonstrates temporal multi-mode capability. This provides a critical building block for scalable, chip-scale quantum repeaters and the quantum internet.
main_problem: The lack of a demonstrated on-chip quantum memory within the thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) platform for telecom-band photons, which is essential for scalable quantum repeaters and networks.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated the first on-chip quantum memory in Er3+-doped TFLN, achieving a storage time of 400 ns with 1.95% efficiency and a time-bin qubit storage fidelity of 96.8%.
method: The study utilizes the Atomic Frequency Comb (AFC) protocol via spectral hole burning in erbium-doped TFLN waveguides, combined with photon-counting measurements and unbalanced Mach-Zehnder interferometry.
model_or_system: An integrated platform consisting of a 5 mm-long x-cut thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) waveguide doped with 0.5 mol% Er3+ ions, operated at cryogenic temperatures (1.6 K) and under a 4 T magnetic field.
key_observables: Optical lifetime (T1), optical coherence time (T2), ground-state lifetime, absorption coefficient, storage efficiency, and quantum state fidelity.
important_parameters: Telecom wavelength (1531.6 nm), storage time of 400 ns, efficiency of 1.95%, and 4-mode temporal multiplexing capability.
figures_summary: Fig 2 shows spectroscopic characterization including absorption, T1, T2, and spectral hole decay; Fig 3 displays the AFC structure, photon time histograms, and multi-mode storage; Fig 4 shows photon-counting histograms for interference analysis.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the motivation for integrated quantum memory, describes the TFLN platform and Er3+ doping, details the spectroscopic characterization of the ions, presents the implementation of the AFC protocol for storage and multiplexing, and concludes with the demonstration of high-fidelity time-bin qubit storage.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates the control of coherence and state preservation in a solid-state integrated platform, specifically addressing the challenges of decoherence and efficient photon-matter interaction in the telecom band.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 (a) Schematic of the integrated quantum memory on the TFLN platform. Quantum states are coupled into and out of the waveguide through grating couplers, the waveguide is doped with Er3+ ions. (b) Optical microscope image of the fabricated grating couplers and the 5 mm-long straight waveguide. The yellow-shaded region is not shown to scale. (c), (d) Scanning electron microscope image of the grating coupler and waveguide. (e) Finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulation of the fundamental transverse-electric (TE) mode profile at 1531.6 nm in the waveguide, showing an overlap of 85.37% between the optical mode and the Er3+-doped TFLN region. The X –Y plane represents the LN crystal...

Fig. 2 (a) Absorption spectrum of Er3+ ions in a 5 mm long TFLN waveguide. The inset shows the simplified energy-level diagram of Er3+ ions in TFLN. (b) Fluorescence decay of the 4I 13/2 state at 1.6 K, with a single exponential fit (blue line) yielding a lifetime of T1 = 2.78 ± 0.02 ms. (c) Two-pulse photon echo (PE) decay of the 4I 15/2 ↔4I 13/2 transition, fitted with an exponential function (green line) giving a coherence time of T2 = 17.48 ± 1.69 µs. (d) Decay of the spectral hole area, fitted using three exponential decay functions. The shortest hole lifetime is estimated to 1.95 ± 1.09 s.

Fig. 3 (a) AFC structure created in Er3+:TFLN chip via spectral hole burning. The teeth spacing ∆is 2.5 MHz, corresponding to storage time of 400 ns. The linewidth of the teeth is γ = 1.03 ± 0.02 MHz. (b) Time histograms of the input photons (red) and the photons retrieved after a storage time of 400 ns (green), measured at an input mean photon number of µin = 0.578. The bin size is 1 ns in this histogram. (c) Time histograms of four temporal modes memory.

Fig. 4 (a), (b) Photon-counting histograms for the retrieved states |e⟩+ |l⟩and |e⟩−|l⟩, where constructive (destructive) interference is shown in red (blue). The interference peak corresponds to the central peak, and the average input photon number is µin = 1.610. (c) Total fidelity as a function of the photon number per input qubit. The fidelity achieved by the classical measure-and-prepare strategy is indicated by the green line (see Supplementary Eq. (2) for details). The orange points show the fidelities obtained with the memory; the full dataset is summarized in Supplementary Table S1.
